title: Features: What a Model Actually Looks At
concept: A feature is a measurable piece of information about an example — like a house's size or number of bedrooms — that a model uses as an input to make its guess.

exercises.prompt: You're trying to guess how much a used car is worth. Which of these is a measurable detail you could actually use to help guess?

options.text: The car's mileage
A dream you had about the car
How the car makes you feel emotionally

exercises.explain: Right! Mileage is a concrete, measurable detail. Feelings and dreams aren't things a model can measure or use.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model can only make a guess using pieces of information it's actually given as input.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! A model has no access to anything beyond the measurable inputs it's handed — nothing more, nothing less.

cards.title: Guessing a house's price
cards.body: Before guessing a house's price, a real-estate model needs to know things about it: how big is it? How many bedrooms? What neighborhood? These specific, measurable details are what the model actually looks at to make its guess.

cards.title: What is a feature?
cards.body: A feature is a measurable piece of information about an example — like a house's square footage, number of bedrooms, or age — that a model uses as an input when making a guess.

cards.title: Clues for a detective
cards.body: Think of features like clues a detective uses to solve a case: footprint size, time of day, fingerprints. The detective can't act on a hunch that isn't backed by a clue — and a model can't use information that isn't captured as a feature.

cards.title: Features of a house
cards.body: For predicting a house's price, useful features might include: square footage, number of bedrooms, number of bathrooms, age of the house, and distance to the nearest school.

cards.title: More features isn't automatically better
cards.body: It's tempting to think piling on every possible feature always helps. Actually, an irrelevant feature — like the house's street-sign color — can just add noise and confuse the model instead of helping it.

cards.title: Useful vs. useless features
cards.body: Not every piece of information is worth feeding a model:

right.text:
Front door color
Barely relates to price

exercises.prompt: You're building a model to guess how long a pizza delivery will take. Which of these is the best example of a useful feature?

options.text: Distance from the restaurant to the delivery address
The customer's favorite movie
The delivery driver's shoe size
The color of the pizza box

exercises.explain: Exactly! Distance is a measurable detail that's directly relevant to delivery time — a strong feature.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A feature must be something measurable, not just a vague feeling or opinion.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Features are concrete, measurable pieces of information — not vague impressions.

exercises.sentence: A model can only base its guess on the ___ it's given as input, like size or age.
exercises.explain: Features are the measurable inputs a model actually has access to.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a well-chosen feature for guessing a student's test score?

options.text: Hours spent studying for the test
The student's favorite color
The number of clouds in the sky that day

exercises.explain: Correct! Study time plausibly relates to test performance, making it a much more useful feature than an unrelated detail.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Adding an irrelevant feature to a model can sometimes make its guesses worse instead of better.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Irrelevant features can add confusing noise, sometimes actually hurting a model's guesses rather than helping.

exercises.prompt: A team is building a model to guess a car's resale value. They include features like mileage, age, and brand — plus the license plate number as a feature. What's the problem with including the license plate number?

options.text: It's essentially a random, unique label with no real relationship to the car's value, so it just adds noise
License plates are illegal to use in any dataset
It will make the model impossible to train at all
It's actually the single most useful feature for resale value

exercises.explain: Exactly! A near-random, unique identifier like a license plate doesn't meaningfully relate to value — it's noise, not a useful feature.

exercises.prompt: Two models guess apartment rent prices. Model A uses square footage, number of bedrooms, and neighborhood. Model B uses only the tenant's favorite pizza topping. Which is more likely to make accurate guesses, and why?

options.text: Model A, because its features are measurable details that genuinely relate to rent price
Model B, because pizza toppings are a fun and unusual feature
Both equally, since any feature works the same way
Model B, because fewer features are always better regardless of relevance

exercises.explain: Right! Features that genuinely relate to the thing you're predicting lead to far better guesses than irrelevant ones.
